source_metadata.title_raw: William M. Carroll - A-19-068 - August 20, 2019 - Yreka

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 20, 2019

William M. Carroll
Deputy County Counsel
Office of the County Counsel
County of Siskiyou
P.O. Box 659
Yreka, CA 96097

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-068

Dear Mr. Carroll:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Siskiyou County Planning
Commissioner Danielle Lindler regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a
finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Lindler from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to a proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance," which would authorize youth agricultural
projects and actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry on real property zoned Rural
Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural, given that the Commissioner's family's
agricultural business currently raises and sells pigs as part of a 4-H youth agricultural project on the
Commissioner's five-acre real property zoned Prime Agricultural and Non-Prime Agricultural?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the
Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would
have a material financial effect on any of the Commissioner's financial interests at issue based on
the facts presented.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-068
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a Deputy County Counsel for the County of Siskiyou and the authorized
representative of Planning Commissioner Danielle Lindler. The Planning Commission will soon
consider a resolution recommending that the Board of Supervisors enact a proposed Multispecies
Zoning Ordinance, which would authorize additional uses of real property within the County zoned
Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural (collectively, the "RR/AG
Parcels"). Specifically, the Ordinance would authorize an owner of an RR/AG Parcel to engage in
actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry, and to conduct youth agricultural projects,
such as "4-H projects raising livestock," on the RR/AG Parcel.

4-H is an international association of organizations dedicated to fostering youth
development through experiential learning programs. In the United States, 4-H has been
traditionally associated with agricultural activities.

The Commissioner owns a five-acre parcel of real property within the County zoned Prime
Agricultural and Non-Prime Agricultural, that RR/AG Parcel is worth at least $2,000, and she and
her family currently run a business raising and selling pigs from that RR/AG Parcel as part of a 4-H
youth agricultural project. For six months of the year, the business raises two to three pigs on the
Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel for the 4-H project. The Commissioner conservatively estimates
that her family business generates annual revenues of $9,600 raising pigs for the 4-H project with
annual expenses of $3,600.

You state that the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel is particularly suitable for pasture grazing
operations because its 3.5 acres of pasture has good soil and water on site, and that the use of that
parcel for pasture grazing operations could be more profitable than the parcel's existing use. The
Commissioner, however, has no intention of establishing a commercial pasture grazing operation on
her RR/AG Parcel in the foreseeable future. Furthermore, establishing a commercial pasture grazing
operation on the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel would require the construction of infrastructure
including fencing.

Because the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance would authorize additional uses of RR/AG
Parcels, you conservatively estimate that enactment of the Ordinance would result in a slight
increase in the market value of the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that
may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103.
With respect to decisions relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance, Commissioner Lindler has
the following interests based on the facts presented:

File No. A-19-068
Page No. 3

• A business interest in her and her family's agricultural business assuming she has an investment in that business worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)), or holds a management position with, or is a director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of, that business (see Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest in her five-acre RR/AG Parcel which she owns and is worth at least $2,000. (See Section 87103(b).)

• A source of income interest in her and her family's agricultural business because she has aggregated more than $500 in income from that business in the past 12 months. (See Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in her personal finances. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. In addition, a business interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting the business as described in Regulation 18702.1(a)(1), and a real property interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting the property as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's business interest, and Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's source of income interest. The Fair Political Practices Commission repealed the former versions of Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.3, and adopted new versions of those regulations, at its regular meeting on July 23, 2019. The analyses below apply those regulations as updated.

The Commissioner's Agricultural Business

Under Regulation 18702.1(a)(2), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity's assets and liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000, or five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues, and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000. Likewise, a financial effect on a business entity is material if the decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $

File No. A-19-068
Page No. 4

250,000, or one percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $
2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)).²

The Commissioner conservatively estimates that her family's agricultural business has
annual revenues of approximately $9,600 with expenses of approximately $3,600. Therefore, the
decisions relating to the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance would have a material financial effect on
that business if those decisions may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross
revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, of at least $10,000 or a change in expenses of at
least $2,500.

Given the limited nature of the Commissioner's family's current agriculture business, there
is no indication that the Commissioner intends to establish a more substantial commercial pasture
grazing operation on her RR/AG Parcel at this time, the cost of infrastructure construction
necessary to facilitate that parcel's transition to a commercial pasture grazing operation, and that
any future business operations will be limited by the fact that the parcel is only five acres (with only
three and a half acres of pasture), it does not appear reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to
the Ordinance would result in an increase or decrease of the annual gross revenues, or the assets or
liabilities, of the Commissioner's family's agricultural business which would be material under
Regulation 18702.1(a)(2), or a change in the business's expenses meeting the materiality threshold
of Regulation 18702.1(a)(3). Therefore, those decisions would not have a material financial effect
on that business.

The Commissioner's Real Property Parcel

Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's
effect on an official's real property interest, and subdivision (a)(2) of that regulation provides that a
decision's effect on an official's real property interest is material if the decision determines the
parcel's zoning or rezoning, other than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in
that category.

The Multispecies Zoning Ordinance applies to all RR/AG Parcels within the County. In
other words, the Ordinance would authorize both youth agricultural projects and actively managed

² Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
business interest is material if the official knows or has reason to know that the business has an interest in real property
and there is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a substantial effect on that interest. Because the
Commissioner also has a real property interest in her RR/AG Parcel separate from her family business's real property
interest in that parcel, we apply Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether the effect of decisions relating to the
Multispecies Zoning Ordinance would have a disqualifying effect on the Commissioner's real property interest in her
RR/AG Parcel.

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable
effect on an official's source of income interest, and subdivision (a)(4) provides that a decision's effect on such an
interest is material if the source is a business that will be financially affected under the materiality standards of
Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member, and subdivision
(c) of that regulation provides that if the decision affects the official's interest in a business or a real property,
materiality is determined under Regulation 18702.1 or Regulation 18702.2. Therefore, we apply Regulations 18702.1
and 18702.2 to determine whether decisions relating to the Ordinance would have a disqualifying effect on the
Commissioner's interests at issue.

File No. A-19-068
Page No. 5

pasture grazing of hogs or poultry on all parcels designated Rural Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural within the County. Therefore, the effect of decisions relating to the Ordinance on the Commissioner's real property interest in her RR/AG Parcel would not be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2).

Thus, the Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the Ordinance based on the facts presented.³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:aja

___________________________

³ You conservatively estimate, however, that the Multispecies Zoning Ordinance would result in a slight increase in the market value of the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel due to the Ordinance's authorization of additional uses of that parcel. We note that if it becomes apparent that the decisions relating to the Ordinance would increase the market value of the Commissioner's RR/AG Parcel to an extent that it becomes reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on that parcel, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions. (See Sections 87100 and 87103.)

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Lindler from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance," which would authorize youth agricultural

Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural, given that the Commissioner's family's 
agricultural business currently raises and sells pigs as part of a 4-H youth agricultural project on the 
Commissioner's five-acre real property zoned Prime Agricultural and Non-Prime Agricultural?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would 
have a material financial effect on any of the Commissioner's financial interests at issue based on 
the facts presented.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Lindler from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed "Multispecies Zoning Ordinance," which would authorize youth agricultural 
projects and actively managed pasture grazing of hogs or poultry on real property zoned Rural 
Residential, Prime Agricultural, or Non-Prime Agricultural, given that the Commissioner's family's 
agricultural business currently raises and sells pigs as part of a 4-H youth agricultural project on the 
Commissioner's five-acre real property zoned Prime Agricultural and Non-Prime Agricultural?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Multispecies Zoning Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would 
have a material financial effect on any of the Commissioner's financial interests at issue based on 
the facts presented.
